# Preface {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-caution}
## Report bugs

This tutorial is nearly fully LLM-generated. Proceed with caution. We appreciate [bug reports](https://github.com/panproto/panproto/issues).
:::

panproto is a schematic version control system parameterized over algebraic theories. It derives migration validation, data transformation, bidirectional lenses, and breaking-change detection from the pair of theories that define a schema language: one theory for the shape of schemas, one for the shape of data. The same algorithms apply to ATProto Lexicons, SQL DDL, Protobuf, GraphQL, and [76 other protocols](appendices/C-protocol-catalog.qmd). This tutorial teaches you how to use them.

## What this tutorial covers

The tutorial is organized in seven parts:

- **Part I**: the schema migration problem, concretely.
- **Part II**: the structural foundation (schemas as graphs, theories, protocols as pairs of theories).
- **Part III**: migration end-to-end (writing, validating, executing migrations; bidirectional lenses; CI integration).
- **Part IV**: data lifting, custom protocols, schema version control, cross-protocol translation.
- **Part V**: naming, automatic migration discovery, the building-block theory library, testing.
- **Part VI**: automated lenses (protolenses, automatic lens generation, symmetric lenses).
- **Part VII**: data versioning and value-dependent transforms.

## Who this is for

You write software that handles structured data. You know what a Protobuf message, a SQL table, or a JSON Schema looks like. We introduce every algebraic concept as we need it; formal foundations are collected in [Appendix A](appendices/A-formal-foundations.qmd).

## Installation

::: {.panel-tabset}

### TypeScript

```bash
npm install @panproto/core
```

### Rust

```toml
[dependencies]
panproto-core = "0.6"
```

### Python

```bash
pip install panproto
```

:::

Code examples use the panproto TypeScript SDK (`@panproto/core`), Rust API (`panproto-core`), and Python SDK (`panproto`). All code is pulled from source files using the [`include-code-files`](https://github.com/quarto-ext/include-code-files) Quarto extension, so what you see matches the implementation.
